perturbation.original_text: The Joint Venturers shall maintain adequate books and records to be kept of all the Joint Venture activities and affairs conducted pursuant to the terms of this Agreement. All direct costs and expenses, which shall include any insurance costs in connection with the distribution of the Products or operations of the Joint Venture, or if the business of the Joint Venture requires additional office facilities than those now presently maintained by each Joint Venturer, such item shall be paid by the Joint Venture. The fiscal year of the Joint Venture shall be the calendar year, and shall use the cash basis of accounting. If requested by a Joint Venturer, the Joint Venture books and records shall be audited as of the close of each year by an independent accountant acceptable to both Joint Venturers. All books and records of every kind and character, of the Joint Venture, and other information, shall be kept at the principal office of the Joint Venture, or at such other place or places as may be agreed upon by the Joint Venturers, and shall be fully available to each Joint Venturer or his duly authorized representative, all at reasonable times. The books of the Joint Venture shall represent the complete record and report of business operations, including a balance sheet and income and expense statements reflecting all receipts and disbursements of the Joint Venture, and such reports shall be submitted to the Joint Venturers on a regular basis.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venturers shall maintain books and records of the Joint Venture activities and affairs. All direct costs and expenses shall be paid by the Joint Venture. The fiscal year of the Joint Venture shall be the calendar year. If requested by a Joint Venturer, the Joint Venture books and records may be audited. Books and records of every kind and character, of the Joint Venture, shall be kept at the principal office of the Joint Venture, or at such other place or places as may be agreed upon by the Joint Venturers, and shall be available to each Joint Venturer or his duly authorized representative. The books of the Joint Venture shall represent the record and report of business operations, and such reports shall be submitted to the Joint Venturers.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for 'adequate' books and records, eliminates 'insurance costs' as a required expense, removes the required use of 'cash basis accounting', and removes the requirement that the audit be performed by an 'independent accountant acceptable to both Joint Venturers.' Further, it removes the requirement that the balance sheet and income/expense statements reflect all receipts and disbursements. This weakens financial transparency and controls, creating potential legal risks.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law requires businesses to maintain accurate and complete financial records. This includes the proper recording of all transactions, adhering to accounting principles, and ensuring transparency for all parties involved. The omission of key requirements, such as the cash basis of accounting and independent audits, puts the joint venture at risk of non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 Pa.C.S. § 1508 mandates the maintenance of proper records for business entities in Pennsylvania. By removing the explicit requirements for 'adequate' records, 'cash basis of accounting', and audits by an 'independent accountant,' the modified text creates a legal risk. It contradicts the spirit and intention of the law by allowing for potentially incomplete or inaccurate financial reporting, which can violate the rights of the joint venturers to have access to accurate information.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes key requirements for financial record-keeping and auditing, which weakens financial transparency and controls. Specifically, it removes the requirements for 'adequate' records, 'cash basis accounting', and audits by an 'independent accountant'. The law explanation states that this contradicts 15 Pa.C.S. § 1508 by allowing for potentially incomplete or inaccurate financial reporting. The omission of these requirements, according to the perturbation explanation, creates potential legal risks. Although there are no snippets to reference, the Perturbation and Law explanations support the determination that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Joint Venturers recognize that both of the Joint Venturers are and will continue to be engaged in the conduct of their respective businesses for their own account. Neither Joint Venturer shall be entitled to compensation for services rendered to the Joint Venture as such, but each Joint Venturer shall be reimbursed for all direct expenses, including travel, office, and all other out-of-pocket expenses incurred in the operation of the affairs of the Joint Venture and the promotion of its businesses. It is agreed that either Joint Venturer shall, except as provided for below, have authority to execute instruments of any character relating to the affairs of the Joint Venture; provided, that without the written consent or approval of both of the Joint Venturers: (i) the Joint Venture shall incur no liability of any sort, nor any indebtedness for borrowed funds; (ii) no assets owned in the name of the Joint Venture be disposed of; and (iii) no commitment to purchase any item for the Joint Venture shall be made.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venturers recognize that both of the Joint Venturers are and will continue to be engaged in the conduct of their respective businesses for their own account. Neither Joint Venturer shall be entitled to compensation for services rendered to the Joint Venture as such. It is agreed that either Joint Venturer shall have authority to execute instruments of any character relating to the affairs of the Joint Venture.
perturbation.explanation: The modification omits the clause requiring reimbursement for 'all direct expenses, including travel, office, and all other out-of-pocket expenses'. Further, it removes the critical safeguards requiring written consent for incurring liability, disposing of assets, and making purchase commitments. In Pennsylvania, partners in a joint venture generally have a right to reimbursement for expenses and strict limitations of authority without the express consent of the partners.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This omission may conflict with Pennsylvania's Revised Uniform Partnership Act, which implies a duty to act in good faith and fair dealing, which can be interpreted as requiring reimbursement for reasonable expenses incurred on behalf of the partnership and strict limitations of authority without the express consent of the partners.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 Pa.C.S. § 8313 covers the rights and duties of partners, including the duty of care and the rights to reimbursement for expenses. By removing the reimbursement clause and limitations of authority, the modified text could be interpreted as violating the implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, potentially leading to legal disputes over unreimbursed expenses or unauthorized actions.
perturbation.location: Section 5. Powers and Authority of the Joint Venturers.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 8313 - Partnership property :: 2015 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2015 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2015 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes Title 15 - CORPORATIONS AND UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATIONS Chapter 83 - General Partnerships Section 8313 - Partnership property 15 PA Cons Stat § 8313 (2015) What's This? § 8313. Partnership property. (a) General rule.-- All property originally brought into the partnership stock or subsequently acquired, by purchase or otherwise, on account of the partnership is partnership property. (b) Acquisition with partnership funds.-- Unless the contrary intention appears, property acquired with partnership funds is partnership property. (c) Property in partnership name.-- Any estate in real property may be acquired in the partnership name. Title so acquired can be conveyed only in the partnership name. (d) Extent of interest acquired.-- A conveyance to a partnership in the partnership name, though without words of inheritance, passes the entire estate of the grantor unless a contrary intent appears. perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a clause requiring reimbursement for expenses and limitations of authority, which is removed in the modified text. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that Pennsylvania law generally provides partners in a joint venture the right to reimbursement for expenses and strict limitations of authority, which has been removed here. The Law Explanation further elaborates that this omission could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, potentially leading to legal disputes. Scraped Law Snippet 2, while not directly addressing reimbursement, indicates that partnership property includes assets acquired on behalf of the partnership, supporting the notion that partners have rights related to partnership assets and operations. Therefore, by omitting the reimbursement clause and limitations on authority, the modified text contradicts standard partnership practices and may violate implied duties, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Distributions may only be made if after any distribution is made, the Joint Venture assets are in excess of all liabilities of the Joint Venture. Each distribution shall be made ratably to the Joint Venturers according to their prorata interest in the Joint Venture as shown in Section 6.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributions may be made at the discretion of the Joint Venturers.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the legally required condition that distributions can only be made if assets exceed liabilities. Additionally, it omits the requirement that distributions be made ratably according to their pro rata interest. This creates potential conflicts with Pennsylvania's Revised Uniform Partnership Act concerning fair and equitable distribution of assets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The omission contradicts the basic principles of partnership law in Pennsylvania, which require equitable distribution of assets and liabilities among partners. Distribution of profits or assets when the joint venture is insolvent or not distributing profits proportionally violates these principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 Pa.C.S. § 8314 covers a partner’s rights and duties with respect to contributions. By removing the requirement for assets to exceed liabilities before distribution, and mandating pro-rata distribution, this potentially violates the rights of the parties by allowing an unfair or illegal distribution.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires distributions to be made only if assets exceed liabilities and ratably according to pro rata interest. The changed text allows distributions at the discretion of the Joint Venturers, removing both requirements. The Perturbation Explanation states this omission creates potential conflicts with Pennsylvania's Revised Uniform Partnership Act concerning fair and equitable asset distribution, and the Law Explanation supports this by stating that 15 Pa.C.S. § 8314 covers a partner’s rights and duties with respect to contributions. This removal of legally required conditions, as mentioned in the Perturbation Explanation, aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, thus creating a legal contradiction. Since the scraped snippets are 'None', the decision is based on the other inputs.
